examples.en: I am a student.
This ramen is famous.
This is a residence card.

nuance: The polite copula that ends most textbook sentences; its plain-form equivalent is だ, which you drop entirely in casual speech (学生だ, or often just 学生). Unlike English "is", です carries politeness, not extra meaning.
caution: です does not conjugate the na-adjective before it — write 静かです, never 静かいです; the "い-adjective + です" pattern (高いです) is politeness only, so never say 高いだ.

peg.ja: 僕はドラえもんです。
peg.source: ドラえもん (Doraemon) — Doraemon (styled)

connection: N / na-adj + じゃありません
nuance: じゃありません is the more colloquial polite form; ではありません is the stiffer, more formal polite form.

examples.en: I am not a shop clerk.
I'm not free today.
This isn't a smoking seat.

caution: Don't attach it to い-adjectives — 高いじゃありません is wrong; the negative of an い-adjective is 高くないです / 高くありません instead.

peg.ja: 別に好きじゃありません。
peg.en: It's not like I like you or anything.
peg.source: とらドラ！(Toradora!) — Taiga (styled)

nuance: As a topic particle は is pronounced "wa", not "ha".

examples.en: The share house is quiet.
I take the train every morning.
Shibuya has a lot of people.

caution: は marks the topic, not the subject — in 象は鼻が長い the は-phrase is what the sentence is *about*, while が marks the real subject; beginners wrongly treat は as an all-purpose subject marker.

peg.ja: 今日はいい天気ですね。
peg.romaji: Kyō wa ii tenki desu ne.
peg.en: As for today, the weather's nice, isn't it?
peg.source: となりのトトロ (My Neighbor Totoro) — Satsuki (styled)

nuance: が marks the grammatical subject, often new or emphasized info; は marks the topic.

examples.en: There is a konbini in front of the station.
Which train is faster?
The train came.

caution: Question words as subjects take が, not は — ask 誰が来ますか, never 誰は来ますか; and answers to "who/what did it" also stay with が.

peg.ja: 鬼が人を食べる。
peg.source: 鬼滅の刃 (Demon Slayer) — Tanjiro (styled)

nuance: As a particle を is pronounced "o".

examples.en: I buy tea at the konbini.
I study Japanese every night.
I buy a ticket at the station.

caution: Motion verbs use を for the path passed through, not an object — 公園を歩く means "walk through the park", so を here is not the thing being acted on.

peg.ja: 心臓を捧げよ！
peg.source: 進撃の巨人 (Attack on Titan) — Erwin

connection: time N + に / place N + に
nuance: に marks a point in time or a destination; で marks the place an action happens.

examples.en: Work starts at seven o'clock.
On Sunday I go to the sento.
I get up at seven.

caution: Only absolute time words take に (3時に, 月曜日に); relative words like 今日・明日・毎日 take NO に — 明日に行きます is wrong.

peg.ja: 六時に起きます。
peg.source: 魔女の宅急便 (Kiki's Delivery Service) — Kiki (styled)

connection: place N + で / tool N + で
nuance: で marks where an action takes place, or the means/tool used to do it.

examples.en: I drink with friends at the izakaya.
I went to the ward office by bus.
I work at a convenience store.

caution: で is where an action happens; に is where something exists or a destination — 図書館で勉強する (study *at*) vs 図書館にいる (be *at*). Swapping them is the classic beginner slip.

peg.ja: 公園で遊びます。
peg.source: ドラえもん (Doraemon) — Nobita (styled)

nuance: As a direction particle へ is pronounced "e".

examples.en: I want to go to Hokkaido.
Next week I'll climb Mt. Fuji.
I go to the ward office.

caution: へ only marks direction toward a place; it cannot mark a point in time or an existence location, so use に (not へ) for arrival-at and for に-time expressions.

peg.ja: 新しい島へ行くぞ！
peg.en: We're headed to a new island!
peg.source: ONE PIECE — Luffy (styled)

nuance: と lists items exhaustively ("A and B, that's all"); や lists a few examples.

examples.en: I'll go to the konbini and the supermarket.
I drank at an izakaya with a friend.
I order beer and tea.

caution: と only joins nouns ("A and B"); it never joins two sentences or clauses — for "and then" between actions use て-form or それから, not と.

peg.ja: 魚と野菜を買います。
peg.source: サザエさん (Sazae-san) — Sazae (styled)

nuance: や implies "A and B, among others" — the list is not complete.

examples.en: There's tea, milk and such in the fridge.
In Akihabara I looked at manga, games and the like.
In the share-house there are Indians, Thais, and so on.

caution: や joins nouns only and implies an incomplete list, so it clashes with 全部 or "exactly these"; when you mean a closed, exhaustive list use と instead.

peg.ja: おもちゃやお菓子が欲しい！
peg.en: I want toys and snacks and stuff!
peg.source: クレヨンしんちゃん (Crayon Shin-chan) — Shinnosuke (styled)

examples.en: My room is on the second floor.
I bought a Japanese book.
This is my friend's umbrella.

nuance: Links two nouns as possessor-to-possessed or modifier-to-head (私の本, 日本語の本); the order is opposite to English — the modifier comes first, so 日本語の本 is "a book *of* Japanese", i.e. a Japanese book.
caution: Don't insert の between a na-adjective and its noun — it's 静かな部屋 with な, not 静かの部屋; の links nouns, な links na-adjectives.

peg.ja: 君の名は。
peg.source: 君の名は。(Your Name) — film title

nuance: も replaces は or が (not just adds to them) to mean "also / too".

examples.en: I like the sento too.
Tomorrow is an early shift too.
I study Japanese too.

caution: も replaces は/が/を outright — say 私も行きます, never 私はも or 私もは; but with に/で/へ it stacks on (東京にも).

peg.ja: 俺もラーメンが大好きだ！
peg.romaji: Ore mo rāmen ga daisuki da!
peg.source: NARUTO — Naruto (styled)

nuance: か at the end of a sentence turns it into a question; no question mark needed.

examples.en: Where is the toilet?
Does this train go to Shinjuku?
How much is this?

caution: In plain/casual speech か is usually dropped and replaced by rising intonation (行く？); tacking か onto plain form (行くか？) sounds blunt or challenging, not neutral.

peg.ja: 犯人は誰ですか。
peg.source: 名探偵コナン (Detective Conan) — Conan (styled)

nuance: ね seeks agreement or shared feeling — "right?", "isn't it?".

examples.en: It's hot today, isn't it?
This ramen is delicious, isn't it?
This izakaya is cheap, isn't it?

caution: ね assumes the listener shares or will agree with your view; use it to state something the other person can't know (like new info about yourself), and it sounds like you're fishing for agreement you have no right to expect.

peg.ja: このケーキ、おいしいですね。
peg.en: This cake is delicious, isn't it?
peg.source: けいおん！(K-On!) — Yui (styled)

nuance: よ tells the listener something new or emphasizes your point — "you know", "I'm telling you".

examples.en: The last train has already gone, you know.
That izakaya over there is cheap, you know.
That train is an express, you know.

caution: よ asserts information you assume the listener lacks; using it on something obvious to them can sound pushy or condescending, so don't reflexively end every statement with よ.

peg.ja: 月に代わって、お仕置きよ！
peg.en: In the name of the moon, I'll punish you!
peg.source: 美少女戦士セーラームーン (Sailor Moon) — Usagi

examples.en: I drink coffee every morning.
I'll meet a friend tomorrow.
Every morning I ride the Yamanote line.

nuance: The polite present/future form — it covers both habitual actions and future plans (毎日飲みます = "I drink daily" and 明日行きます = "I'll go tomorrow"); Japanese has no separate future tense. Its plain equivalent is the dictionary form.
caution: ます attaches to the ます-stem (i-row), not the dictionary form — it's 飲みます from 飲む, never 飲むます; irregulars are します and 来ます (きます).

peg.ja: 毎日ベースを練習します。
peg.source: けいおん！(K-On!) — Mio (styled)

examples.en: I won't drink alcohol today.
I don't eat natto.
I don't drink alcohol.

nuance: The polite negative of ます, covering "don't / won't" (今日は飲みません). It shares the same ます-stem as the affirmative — you just swap ます for ません; the plain-form counterpart is 〜ない.
caution: ません is the negative of ます (present/future); its past is ませんでした, so "didn't eat" is 食べませんでした, never 食べませんかった.

peg.ja: 宿題はまだしません。
peg.en: I won't do my homework yet.
peg.source: ちびまる子ちゃん (Chibi Maruko-chan) — Maruko (styled)

examples.en: I went to the ward office yesterday.
I climbed Mt. Fuji last week.
Yesterday I met a friend in Shibuya.

nuance: The polite past of ます — one form covers both English "did" and "have done" (食べました = "ate" / "have eaten"). Context, not the verb, tells them apart. The plain equivalent is the た-form.
caution: Its negative is not ましたじゃない — the polite past negative is a separate form, ませんでした (行きませんでした = "didn't go").

peg.ja: ルパンを逮捕しました。
peg.source: ルパン三世 (Lupin III) — Zenigata (styled)

examples.en: I didn't go to work yesterday.
I didn't make the last train.
The train didn't come.

nuance: The polite past negative ("didn't / hasn't") — built by adding でした after the negative ません, literally "was not (doing)". Its plain-form counterpart is 〜なかった.
caution: It only negates verbs; for a noun/na-adjective in the polite past negative you need じゃありませんでした (学生じゃありませんでした), not ませんでした.

peg.ja: 昨日、勉強しませんでした。
peg.source: ドラえもん (Doraemon) — Nobita (styled)

nuance: The plain (casual) negative; ない conjugates like an い-adjective.

examples.en: I'm not going anywhere today.
I don't eat anything in the morning.
I'm not going to my part-time job today.

caution: Only する→しない and 来る→こない are irregular; for regular u-verbs the final -u becomes -a-nai (飲む→飲まない), and verbs ending in う go to わ (買う→買わない, never 買あない).

peg.ja: 俺は絶対に諦めない。
peg.source: NARUTO — Naruto (styled)

examples.en: I didn't sleep yesterday.
I didn't take an umbrella with me.
I didn't sleep at all yesterday.

nuance: The plain past negative — take the 〜ない form and, because ない conjugates like an い-adjective, change ない→なかった (行かない→行かなかった). It's the casual equivalent of ませんでした.
caution: Because ない acts like an い-adjective, the past is なかった (行かなかった), never 行かないでした; ない never takes でした.

peg.ja: 今日は宿題をしなかった。
peg.en: I didn't do my homework today.
peg.source: ドラえもん (Doraemon) — Nobita (styled)

nuance: Expresses an action in progress or a resulting state; casual いる, polite います.

examples.en: I'm waiting for the train now.
I'm studying Japanese every night.
My friend is working at a convenience store.

caution: With change-of-state verbs (行く, 結婚する, 死ぬ) ている marks the resulting *state*, not an action in progress — 行っている means "has gone / is there now", not "is going".

peg.ja: お前はもう死んでいる。
peg.source: 北斗の拳 (Fist of the North Star) — Kenshiro

examples.en: Please wait here.
Please say it one more time.
Please write your name on this form.

nuance: A polite request or instruction ("please do…"), built on the て-form. It's firm and direct — fine for teachers, staff, or signs — but toward superiors soften it with 〜ていただけますか. Its negative is ないでください.
caution: It attaches to the て-form, so get the te-form right — 待ってください (待つ), not 待ちてください; irregular te-forms (して, 来て/きて) carry over.

peg.ja: ドラえもん、助けてください！
peg.source: ドラえもん (Doraemon) — Nobita (styled)

examples.en: Please don't take photos here.
Please don't make phone calls on the train.
Please don't close the door.

nuance: The negative request — "please don't do…" — formed from the plain negative stem plus でください. It's the direct counterpart of てください for prohibitions you're asking of someone.
caution: Use the ない-stem + でください (撮らないでください), not the て-form; and don't confuse it with なくてもいい ("you don't have to"), which permits rather than forbids.

peg.ja: 私を忘れないでください。
peg.source: 千と千尋の神隠し (Spirited Away) — Chihiro (styled)

examples.en: Let's eat ramen together.
Let's head home now.
Let's do hanami together.

nuance: A polite "let's…" that proposes a shared action the speaker assumes both parties want (一緒に行きましょう). Because it presumes agreement, ませんか is the gentler way to *invite* someone who hasn't agreed yet.
caution: ましょう assumes the other person is already on board; to actually invite (and let them decline) use ませんか — leading with ましょう on an unshared plan can sound presumptuous.

peg.ja: 一緒にお茶をしましょう。
peg.source: けいおん！(K-On!) — Tsumugi (styled)

examples.en: Shall I open the window?
Where shall we meet?
Shall I carry your bags?

nuance: Adds か to ましょう to *offer* ("shall I…?") or *check* a shared plan ("shall we…?"). 窓を開けましょうか offers help; どこで会いましょうか asks the group to decide. The subject (I vs we) comes from context.
caution: ましょうか offers to do something / checks a joint plan; it is not a yes-no invitation — to invite someone to join *you*, use ませんか instead.

peg.ja: 窓を開けましょうか。
peg.source: ドラえもん (Doraemon) — Doraemon (styled)

nuance: A softer, more polite invitation than ましょう — "would you like to…?".

examples.en: Won't you go to an izakaya tonight?
Would you like to go to a sento this weekend?
Won't you go for a drink tonight?

caution: This is an invitation, not a real negative question — 行きませんか means "won't you go? (let's)", so answering はい means "yes, let's", not "yes, I won't".

peg.ja: 一緒に部活をしませんか。
peg.romaji: Issho ni bukatsu o shimasen ka.
peg.en: Won't you join the club with me?
peg.source: 涼宮ハルヒの憂鬱 (Haruhi Suzumiya) — Haruhi (styled)

meaning: do things like A and B
connection: V-たり + V-たり + する
nuance: Lists representative actions, not a full or ordered sequence — "do things like A and B".

examples.en: On my day off I do things like cleaning and laundry.
On weekends I do things like watch anime and read manga.
On weekends I do things like cleaning and taking walks.

caution: Don't forget the final する — it's 〜たり〜たりする, and the tense/politeness lives on that する (…したりしました), not on the たり verbs themselves.

peg.ja: 休みの日は、読んだり、寝たりします。
peg.romaji: Yasumi no hi wa, yondari, netari shimasu.
peg.en: On my days off I do things like reading and sleeping.
peg.source: サザエさん (Sazae-san) — Sazae (styled)

connection: V-ない stem + なければなりません
nuance: Drop the ない and add なければなりません. Casual short form: なきゃ.

examples.en: I have to get up early tomorrow.
I have to submit documents at the ward office.
I have to pay the rent.

caution: It literally means "if you don't, it won't do", so the two negatives cancel to a positive obligation ("must do") — don't read the ない as making it negative.

peg.ja: 戦わなければならない。
peg.source: 進撃の巨人 (Attack on Titan) — Eren (styled)

connection: V-ない stem + なくてもいい
nuance: Drop the ない and add なくてもいい to say something isn't necessary.

examples.en: I don't have to go to work today.
You don't have to take off your shoes.
I don't have to work tomorrow.

caution: This is "don't have to", the opposite of "must not" — contrast it with てはいけません (forbidden); 食べなくてもいい means eating is optional, not banned.

peg.ja: 泣かなくてもいいよ。
peg.source: となりのトトロ (My Neighbor Totoro) — Satsuki (styled)

examples.en: I can read a little kanji.
You can withdraw money at the konbini.
I can speak Japanese.

nuance: Expresses ability or permission by nominalizing a dictionary-form verb with こと ("the thing of doing X is possible"). It's the analytic alternative to the potential form (泳げます) and is often felt as slightly more formal or explicit.
caution: It attaches to the dictionary form, not the ます-stem — 泳ぐことができます, never 泳ぎことができます or 泳げることができます.

peg.ja: 私は空を飛ぶことができます。
peg.romaji: Watashi wa sora o tobu koto ga dekimasu.
peg.en: I can fly through the sky.
peg.source: 魔女の宅急便 (Kiki's Delivery Service) — Kiki (styled)

nuance: の nominalizes the verb so it can be the thing you like; 好き is a na-adjective.

examples.en: I like going to the sento.
I like reading books on the train.
I like taking photos.

caution: What you like is marked by が, not を — it's 読むのが好き, never 読むのを好き, because 好き is an adjective describing that the reading "is likeable".

peg.ja: 漫画を読むのが好きです。
peg.romaji: Manga o yomu no ga suki desu.
peg.source: ちびまる子ちゃん (Chibi Maruko-chan) — Maruko (styled)

nuance: 上手 (skilled) is a na-adjective; usually used to praise others, not yourself.

examples.en: He is good at cooking.
My roommate is good at speaking Japanese.
My friend is good at speaking Japanese.

caution: Avoid saying 私は〜のが上手です about yourself — claiming 上手 sounds boastful; describe your own skill with できます or 得意 (still cautiously), and reserve 上手 for praising others.

peg.ja: 私は料理を作るのが上手です。
peg.romaji: Watashi wa ryōri o tsukuru no ga jōzu desu.
peg.source: ドラえもん (Doraemon) — Shizuka (styled)

nuance: 欲しい is for wanting THINGS; use 〜たい for wanting to DO something.

examples.en: I want a new bicycle.
In winter I want a warm coat.
I want my own room.

caution: 欲しい describes the speaker's own desire — don't use it flatly for a third person ("he wants…"); for others you need 欲しがっている.

peg.ja: おれは仲間が欲しい！
peg.source: ONE PIECE — Luffy (styled)

nuance: Attaches to a verb stem or an adjective stem (drop final い/な); means "too" / "over-".

examples.en: This ramen is too spicy.
I drank too much at the izakaya yesterday.
This room's rent is too high.

caution: すぎる is always negative/excessive ("too much", a problem) — it is not an intensifier like とても; 高すぎる means "too expensive (bad)", not just "very expensive".

peg.ja: このカレー、辛すぎます！
peg.source: クレヨンしんちゃん (Crayon Shin-chan) — Shinnosuke (styled)

nuance: Adds an explanatory nuance — giving a reason or background. のです is the formal form.

examples.en: It's that I have a headache.
The thing is, I missed the last train.
(It's that) the train was delayed.

caution: Nouns and na-adjectives need な before んです (学生なんです, 静かなんです), not だ — 学生だんです is wrong; only verbs and い-adjectives attach んです directly.

peg.ja: これは事故じゃないんです。
peg.romaji: Kore wa jiko ja nai n desu.
peg.en: The thing is, this isn't an accident.
peg.source: 名探偵コナン (Detective Conan) — Conan (styled)

nuance: The clause before と思います stays in plain form. Some lists place this at N4; the lower level wins.

examples.en: I think it will be sunny tomorrow.
I think this shop is cheap.
I think Tokyo is convenient.

caution: The と-clause stays in plain form even in polite speech — 行くと思います, never 行きますと思います; and と思います states the speaker's own opinion, not a general fact.

peg.ja: 明日も来ると思います。
peg.en: I think she'll come tomorrow too.
peg.source: 涼宮ハルヒの憂鬱 (Haruhi Suzumiya) — Kyon (styled)

nuance: Expresses probability or a guess; with rising intonation it seeks confirmation.

examples.en: It will probably rain tomorrow.
Hokkaido is probably cold.
It'll probably be crowded this weekend.

caution: Don't stack it on です — it's 高いでしょう / 学生でしょう, never 高いですでしょう; でしょう already carries the polite copula.

peg.ja: もうすぐ来るでしょう。
peg.source: となりのトトロ (My Neighbor Totoro) — Satsuki (styled)

connection: まだ + V-て + いません
nuance: まだ pairs with the negative ていません to mean "not yet (done)".

examples.en: I haven't eaten lunch yet.
I haven't gotten my residence card yet.
I haven't gone to the ward office yet.

caution: まだ ("not yet") takes ていません, not the plain past negative — まだ食べていません, not まだ食べませんでした; the latter wrongly says "still didn't eat".

peg.ja: まだ宿題をやっていません。
peg.en: I haven't done my homework yet.
peg.source: ちびまる子ちゃん (Chibi Maruko-chan) — Maruko (styled)

nuance: もう pairs with the past ました to mean "already (done)".

examples.en: I've already eaten dinner.
I've already opened a bank account.
I already paid the rent.

caution: This もう means "already" with an affirmative past (もう食べました); a different もう + negative means "no longer" (もう食べません), so watch the polarity.

peg.ja: もう三時だよ。
peg.source: ドラえもん (Doraemon) — Doraemon (styled)

examples.en: It takes about ten minutes to the station.
I save about 30,000 yen every month.
I waited about three hours.

nuance: Marks an approximate quantity, duration, or degree ("about", 三十分ぐらい, 千円ぐらい). Interchangeable with ごろ only in feel, not use: ぐらい is for amounts/spans, ごろ for a single clock point. ぐらい and くらい are the same word.
caution: Use ぐらい for amounts and durations (10分ぐらい); for an approximate *point* in time use ごろ — 3時ぐらい is loose, 3時ごろ is the natural choice.

peg.ja: 駅まで十分ぐらいです。
peg.en: It's about ten minutes to the station.
peg.source: サザエさん (Sazae-san) — Sazae (styled)

nuance: ごろ attaches to a POINT in time (7時ごろ); ぐらい attaches to an amount (10分ぐらい).

examples.en: I get up around seven every morning.
The train comes around eight thirty.
Let's meet around three in Shibuya.

caution: ごろ attaches only to a point in time, so don't say 10分ごろ for a duration — a length of time takes ぐらい (10分ぐらい).

peg.ja: 八時ごろ起きました。
peg.source: ドラえもん (Doraemon) — Nobita (styled)

examples.en: Today I only bought water.
I can only read hiragana.
I only have 1000 yen in my wallet.

nuance: Limits to "only / just" and, unlike しか, pairs with an affirmative verb (一人だけ来た). It carries a neutral or even positive "that's enough" feel, whereas しか〜ない stresses the shortfall ("only, and that's too few").
caution: だけ takes a positive verb (一人だけ来た); if you switch to the しか construction you must make the verb negative — the two are not interchangeable word-for-word.

peg.ja: 少しだけ食べます。
peg.source: けいおん！(K-On!) — Tsumugi (styled)

nuance: しか always pairs with a NEGATIVE verb but the meaning is positive-limiting: "only".

examples.en: I only have 100 yen in my wallet.
I can only take Sundays off.
Today I only have 1000 yen.

caution: しか obligatorily requires a negative verb even though the meaning is "only" — 千円しかない = "have only 1000 yen"; 千円しかある is ungrammatical.

peg.ja: 前に進むしかない！
peg.en: We have no choice but to move forward!
peg.source: ONE PIECE — Luffy (styled)

connection: A + より + B + のほうが

examples.en: The bicycle is faster than the train.
I like winter more than summer.
The train is faster than the bus.

nuance: The standard comparison frame: より marks the losing/standard item and (の)ほうが marks the one that wins on the quality (電車より車のほうが速い). Word order is flexible, but ほうが always sits with the item that "has more" of the trait.
caution: より marks the thing being surpassed (the standard), so AよりBのほうが means B > A — beginners often flip it and mark the winner with より by mistake.

peg.ja: 勉強より昼寝のほうがいい。
peg.romaji: Benkyō yori hirune no hō ga ii.
peg.en: A nap is better than studying.
peg.source: ドラえもん (Doraemon) — Nobita (styled)

connection: group + の中で + N + がいちばん

examples.en: Of all the cities in Japan, I like Tokyo the most.
At this restaurant, the ramen is the cheapest.
Among Tokyo's stations, Shinjuku is the biggest.

nuance: The superlative: 〜の中で names the group, がいちばん marks the top member (クラスの中で田中さんがいちばん背が高い). いちばん is just the adverb "most/number one" placed before the adjective — no -est ending exists.
caution: Use の中で (not で alone) to frame the group, and remember いちばん is an adverb before the adjective — 高いちばん is wrong, it's いちばん高い.

peg.ja: クラスでオラがいちばん元気だ！
peg.romaji: Kurasu de ora ga ichiban genki da!
peg.en: I'm the most energetic in class!
peg.source: クレヨンしんちゃん (Crayon Shin-chan) — Shinnosuke (styled)

examples.en: When I have free time, I go to the sento.
When I came to Japan, I went to the ward office.
When the trains are crowded, I walk.

nuance: Marks "when / at the time of" and the tense of the verb before とき matters: 行くとき (dict) = "when about to/on the way to go", 行ったとき (past) = "when I had gone/arrived". Nouns link with の (子供のとき).
caution: The tense on the verb before とき is relative to the main clause, not the sentence — 行くとき写真を撮った means "took a photo *on the way* there", while 行ったとき means "*after* arriving".

peg.ja: 小さいとき、トトロに会った。
peg.en: When I was little, I met Totoro.
peg.source: となりのトトロ (My Neighbor Totoro) — Satsuki (styled)

nuance: The verb before あとで is always PAST plain form (食べたあとで), even in a present-tense sentence.

examples.en: After work is over, I go to the izakaya.
After eating, I wash the dishes.
After getting off the train, I meet a friend at the station.

caution: あとで pins strict sequence and takes the past た-form regardless of sentence tense; don't confuse it with てから, which more strongly stresses that X finishes before Y begins.

peg.ja: ご飯を食べたあとで、散歩します。
peg.romaji: Gohan o tabeta ato de, sanpo shimasu.
peg.en: After eating, I take a walk.
peg.source: サザエさん (Sazae-san) — Sazae (styled)

examples.en: Please tell me how to buy a ticket.
The way to write kanji is difficult.
I don't understand how to use this ticket machine.

nuance: Attaches to the ます-stem to mean "the way of / how to do" (作り方 = "how to make"), turning the verb into a noun. The following object often switches its を to の: 漢字の書き方 ("the way of writing kanji").
caution: Attach 方 to the ます-stem, not the dictionary form — 使い方 (from 使います), never 使う方; and the object particle typically becomes の (この漢字の書き方).

peg.ja: この道具の使い方が分からない。
peg.romaji: Kono dōgu no tsukaikata ga wakaranai.
peg.en: I don't get how to use this gadget.
peg.source: ドラえもん (Doraemon) — Nobita (styled)

examples.en: I'm going to the konbini to buy a bento.
I'm going to Hokkaido to ski.
I'm going to the convenience store to buy lunch.

nuance: Expresses purpose of movement: ます-stem + に + a motion verb (映画を見に行きます = "go to see a movie"). The に here means "in order to", and only motion verbs (行く/来る/帰る) complete it.
caution: Use the ます-stem before に, not the dictionary form — 買いに行く, never 買うに行く; with a する-noun, the noun alone precedes に (勉強しに → 勉強に行く).

peg.ja: ポケモンを捕まえに行きます。
peg.source: ポケットモンスター (Pokémon) — Satoshi (styled)

examples.en: It's not very cold today.
I don't eat natto very much.
This shop isn't very expensive.

nuance: あまり pairs with a negative to mean "not very / not much" (あまり高くない). Without the negative, あまり(に) instead means "excessively", so the negative verb is what fixes the "not very" reading.
caution: あまり demands a negative predicate for the "not much" sense — あまり高い alone doesn't mean "not very expensive"; you must say あまり高くない.

peg.ja: 私はあまり話さない。
peg.source: エヴァンゲリオン (Evangelion) — Rei (styled)

examples.en: I don't understand this kanji at all.
I couldn't sleep at all last night.
I don't understand Japanese at all.

nuance: ぜんぜん emphasizes a total negative — "not at all" (ぜんぜん分からない). In casual modern speech it also appears with positives to mean "totally fine" (ぜんぜん大丈夫), but for N5 keep it strictly with negatives.
caution: In careful/standard usage ぜんぜん needs a negative — ぜんぜん + affirmative (ぜんぜんいい) is colloquial and best avoided in polite writing or on tests.

peg.ja: そんなの全然面白くない。
peg.source: 涼宮ハルヒの憂鬱 (Haruhi Suzumiya) — Kyon (styled)

examples.en: How about the sento this weekend?
How about a coffee?
How about tomorrow evening?

nuance: Asks for an opinion or offers a suggestion ("How about…? / What do you think of…?"), e.g. コーヒーはどうですか offers coffee. いかがですか is the more polite version used with customers or superiors.
caution: This asks "how about it / your opinion", not "how (by what means)" — for method use どうやって; and don't swap in どうですか to ask literal "how are you doing X".

peg.ja: みんな、ケーキはどうですか。
peg.romaji: Minna, kēki wa dō desu ka.
peg.source: けいおん！(K-On!) — Tsumugi (styled)

examples.en: Please give me this one.
Two draft beers, please.
A plastic bag, please.

nuance: Requests a thing/object ("please give me…", これをください). It asks for a noun; to request an *action*, you need the てください form instead (待ってください).
caution: をください requests a *thing*; to request an *action* use the verb's て-form + ください — 待ってください, not 待つをください.

peg.ja: お水をください。
peg.source: 千と千尋の神隠し (Spirited Away) — Chihiro (styled)

connection: い-adj stem + くなります

examples.en: At night it gets cold.
Japanese has gradually become fun.
My luggage got heavy.

nuance: Expresses "become" for い-adjectives: drop い and add くなります (寒い→寒くなります). It describes a natural change of state; for a deliberate change *you* cause, use くします instead.
caution: Use the く form, not に — it's 大きくなる for い-adjectives; only nouns and na-adjectives take になる (mixing them up, e.g. 寒いになる, is the classic error).

peg.ja: もうすぐ暗くなります。
peg.source: となりのトトロ (My Neighbor Totoro) — Satsuki (styled)

examples.en: Next month I turn 25.
I practiced every day and got good at it.
Next week I turn 21.

nuance: The "become" pattern for nouns and na-adjectives, which link with に (医者になる, 静かになる). Parallel to くなります for い-adjectives; the choice of に vs く depends purely on the word class.
caution: Nouns and na-adjectives take に (元気になる); don't apply this to an い-adjective — 高いになる is wrong, it must be 高くなる.

peg.ja: 海賊王に俺はなる！
peg.en: I'm going to be King of the Pirates!
peg.source: ONE PIECE — Luffy

nuance: の turns a verb into a noun, like adding "-ing" or "the act of" (泳ぐ → 泳ぐの).

examples.en: I like reading books on the train.
Getting up early in the morning is tough.
Working a part-time job is tough.

caution: This の makes a verb into a noun; don't confuse it with こと — の is preferred for concrete, perceived acts (泳ぐのを見る), while abstract facts often take こと.

peg.ja: 歌うのが楽しい！
peg.source: 涼宮ハルヒの憂鬱 (Haruhi Suzumiya) — Haruhi (styled)

examples.en: This ramen is cheap and delicious.
The share house is spacious and bright.
This room is small and old.

nuance: Links an い-adjective to what follows by changing い→くて (高くて広い = "expensive and spacious"). It joins clauses and often implies a cause/reason ("being X, so…"); the tense is carried by the final predicate only.
caution: Only い-adjectives use くて; nouns and na-adjectives link with で (静かで, 学生で), not くて — and いい becomes よくて, never いくて.

peg.ja: トトロは大きくて優しい。
peg.source: となりのトトロ (My Neighbor Totoro) — Satsuki (styled)

examples.en: I'll have the miso ramen.
For a drink, I'll have tea.
I'll have coffee.

nuance: States a decision or choice, most often an order — コーヒーにします = "I'll have the coffee". The に marks the option settled on; contrast にする (you decide) with になる (it becomes so on its own).
caution: にします is "I decide on / I'll choose" (volitional); don't confuse it with になります ("becomes", a natural change) — one is a choice, the other happens by itself.

peg.ja: 私はイチゴのケーキにします。
peg.romaji: Watashi wa ichigo no kēki ni shimasu.
peg.source: けいおん！(K-On!) — Yui (styled)

examples.en: I wonder if it will rain tomorrow.
I wonder if this train stops at Shibuya.
I wonder if this train stops at Shinjuku.

nuance: A softened, polite question expressing wondering or a tentative guess ("I wonder if…", 雨でしょうか). It hedges more than plain ですか, so it sounds gentler and less demanding when asking or confirming.
caution: でしょうか softens a question; it isn't a plain yes/no ですか — using it doesn't make the sentence more certain, it makes it more tentative and polite.

peg.ja: 犯人は誰でしょうか。
peg.en: I wonder who the culprit is.
peg.source: 名探偵コナン (Detective Conan) — Conan (styled)

nuance: Describes doing the main action WITHOUT the first one (朝ご飯を食べないで出た).

examples.en: I left home without eating breakfast.
I went out without taking an umbrella.
I go to work without eating breakfast.

caution: ないで means "without doing" as an accompanying manner; for "because not / instead of" contrast, and for linking negative reasons, なくて is used — 食べないで出た ("left without eating") vs 食べなくて… (reason).

peg.ja: 朝ご飯を食べないで学校へ行った。
peg.romaji: Asagohan o tabenaide gakkō e itta.
peg.en: I went to school without eating breakfast.
peg.source: ちびまる子ちゃん (Chibi Maruko-chan) — Maruko (styled)

nuance: けど is the casual, softer counterpart of が; common in everyday speech.

examples.en: Natto is smelly, but it's delicious.
I want to go, but I don't have time.
This job is tough, but fun.

caution: けど is casual — in polite/written contexts use が or けれども; also けど often just softens a lead-in ("um, about that…"), not always a real "but".

peg.ja: 行きたいけど、お金がない。
peg.en: I want to go, but I have no money.
peg.source: ちびまる子ちゃん (Chibi Maruko-chan) — Maruko (styled)

nuance: This が is the contrastive conjunction ("but"), not the subject particle が.

examples.en: This place is expensive, but it's delicious.
Excuse me, but where is the station?
Excuse me, but I don't know the way.

caution: This conjunctive が means "but" and comes mid-sentence after a full clause — don't confuse it with the subject-marker が, and don't start a new sentence with it the way English does with "But".

peg.ja: 失礼ですが、あなたは誰ですか。
peg.romaji: Shitsurei desu ga, anata wa dare desu ka.
peg.en: Excuse me, but who are you?
peg.source: 名探偵コナン (Detective Conan) — Conan (styled)

examples.en: But I don't have any money today.
But it's raining, so I won't go.
I'm tired. But I still have work.

nuance: A sentence-initial "but / however" that pushes back on what was just said (雨だ。でも行く). It's softer and more conversational than しかし; note the same でも after a noun instead means "even" (子供でも).
caution: Sentence-starting でも ("but") is different from the particle でも meaning "even / or something" (コーヒーでも) — same shape, opposite job; judge by position.

peg.ja: 弱いよ。でも、諦めない。
peg.en: I'm weak. But I won't give up.
peg.source: NARUTO — Naruto (styled)

examples.en: I went to the sento. And then I drank milk.
I cleaned. And then I did laundry too.
I took a shower. And then I went to sleep.

nuance: Joins two sentences as "and (then)", adding a further point or the next event (ご飯を食べた。そして寝た). It links *sentences*; to link nouns within one sentence you still need と or や, not そして.
caution: そして connects whole sentences, not nouns — for "A and B" between two nouns use と/や; そして本 is wrong.

peg.ja: 買い物をした。そして、料理をした。
peg.romaji: Kaimono o shita. Soshite, ryōri o shita.
peg.en: I shopped. And then I cooked.
peg.source: サザエさん (Sazae-san) — Sazae (styled)

examples.en: I went to the ward office. After that, I went to the bank.
I bought bread. And also I bought eggs.
I went to the bank. After that, I did some shopping.

nuance: Sequences events as "after that / and then" and can also add another item ("and also"). Compared with そして, それから more clearly stresses temporal order — one thing, then the next.
caution: それから emphasizes sequence ("and after that"); それで, which looks similar, means "and so / therefore" (a result) — don't swap them.

peg.ja: 宿題をした。それから、遊んだ。
peg.en: I did homework. After that, I played.
peg.source: ドラえもん (Doraemon) — Nobita (styled)

examples.en: Tomorrow is work. That's why I'll go to bed early tonight.
It's cold outside. So I'll wear a coat.
Today's Sunday. So the shops are crowded.

nuance: A sentence-initial "so / that's why" that draws a consequence from the previous statement (雨だ。だから行かない). It's the standalone cousin of clause-final から; ですから is its polite form.
caution: だから is casual/assertive and can sound blunt or accusatory toward superiors; in polite speech soften to ですから or それで.

peg.ja: 子供だから、分からないゾ。
peg.en: I'm a kid, so I don't get it.
peg.source: クレヨンしんちゃん (Crayon Shin-chan) — Shinnosuke (styled)

examples.en: I went to an izakaya together with a friend.
I'll climb Mt. Fuji together with my roommate.
I watched fireworks with my friend.

nuance: Means "together with (a person)", where と marks the companion and 一緒に adds "together" (友達と一緒に行く). The と alone already implies togetherness, so 一緒に just makes it explicit and warmer.
caution: The companion is marked by と, not は — it's 友達と一緒に, never 友達は一緒に; 一緒に by itself just means "together" without naming who.

peg.ja: お姉ちゃんと一緒に行く！
peg.source: となりのトトロ (My Neighbor Totoro) — Mei (styled)

examples.en: I go to the station and buy a ticket.
In the morning I get up and wash my face.
I buy a ticket at the station and board the train.

nuance: The て-form chains actions or clauses ("do X and (then) Y", 起きて、顔を洗って…). Only the FINAL verb shows tense and politeness; the て-verbs stay neutral, and sequence/means/cause is read from context.
caution: Tense sits only on the last verb — 起きて食べました makes the whole chain past; don't try to mark tense on each て-form (起きましたて… is wrong).

peg.ja: 朝起きて、顔を洗う。
peg.en: I get up in the morning and wash my face.
peg.source: サザエさん (Sazae-san) — Sazae (styled)

examples.en: What kind of music do you like?
What kind of room are you looking for?
What kind of room would be good?

nuance: Asks "what kind of…?" and always attaches directly to a noun (どんな音楽 = "what kind of music"). Contrast どの ("which of a set") and どう ("how"); どんな probes the type or character of a thing.
caution: どんな must be followed by a noun (どんな人?), and it asks about *kind/type* — not "which one" (that's どの) and not "how" (that's どう).

peg.ja: どんな証拠がありますか。
peg.en: What kind of evidence is there?
peg.source: 名探偵コナン (Detective Conan) — Conan (styled)

examples.en: After work ends, I do the shopping at the supermarket.
After arriving in Japan, I went to the ward office first.
After washing my hands, I eat.

nuance: Sequences two actions as "after doing X, (then) Y", built on the て-form + から. Compared with たあとで, てから stresses that X must be finished first before Y — the completion is the point, not just the order.
caution: Don't confuse this with the reason から — てから attaches to the て-form and orders events ("after doing X, then Y"), it does not mean "because".

peg.ja: 宿題をしてから、遊びます。
peg.en: After I do my homework, I'll play.
peg.source: ドラえもん (Doraemon) — Nobita (styled)

connection: V-dict + 前に / N + の + 前に
nuance: The verb before 前に is ALWAYS dictionary form, even when the sentence is past tense.

examples.en: Before eating, I wash my hands.
Before going to bed, I check tomorrow's weather.
Before sleeping, I brush my teeth.

caution: The verb before 前に is ALWAYS dictionary form even for past events (食べる前に, never 食べた前に); with a noun you need の (食事の前に).

peg.ja: 寝る前に、本を読みます。
peg.romaji: Neru mae ni, hon o yomimasu.
peg.en: Before bed, I read a book.
peg.source: となりのトトロ (My Neighbor Totoro) — Satsuki (styled)

nuance: With a negative verb it flips to "neither … nor".

examples.en: Both the konbini and the supermarket are close by.
I study both kanji and katakana every day.
Both Shinjuku and Shibuya are crowded.

caution: Both も slots replace は/が/を, and with a negative verb the whole thing flips to "neither…nor" (肉も魚も食べない = "eat neither meat nor fish") — watch the verb's polarity.

peg.ja: 肉も魚も大好きだ！
peg.romaji: Niku mo sakana mo daisuki da!
peg.en: I love both meat and fish!
peg.source: ONE PIECE — Luffy (styled)

connection: V-ます stem + たい(です)
nuance: たい conjugates like an い-adjective: 食べたくない, 食べたかった.

examples.en: I want to eat ramen in Hokkaido.
I want to get good at Japanese.
I want to go to an onsen.

caution: たい states the speaker's own desire; don't use it to report a third person's wish — for "he wants to…" use たがっている instead of 〜たい.

peg.ja: 家に帰りたい。
peg.source: 千と千尋の神隠し (Spirited Away) — Chihiro (styled)

connection: N + が + あります (things) / います (people & animals)
nuance: あります for inanimate things; います for people and animals.

examples.en: There is a sento near the station.
There is a cat in the share house.
There's a big window in the room.

caution: Choose the verb by animacy, not politeness — living things (people, animals) take います, inanimate things take あります; 犬があります is the classic error.

peg.ja: 森に大きな木があります。
peg.romaji: Mori ni ōkina ki ga arimasu.
peg.en: There's a big tree in the forest.
peg.source: となりのトトロ (My Neighbor Totoro) — Mei (styled)

examples.en: May I take a photo here?
Is it OK to use the kitchen?
May I sit here?

nuance: Grants permission — "you may / it's OK to…" (帰ってもいいです). Built on the て-form; its negative-permission partner is なくてもいい ("don't have to"), and its prohibition opposite is てはいけません.
caution: This grants permission ("may"), so don't confuse it with its opposite てはいけません ("must not"); also its negative form なくてもいい means "don't have to", not "may not".

peg.ja: これを借りてもいい？
peg.source: ドラえもん (Doraemon) — Nobita (styled)

examples.en: You must not talk on the phone inside the train.
You must not park bicycles here.
You must not take photos here.

nuance: A firm prohibition — "must not / may not" (ここでタバコを吸ってはいけません). Built on the て-form + は; in casual speech ては often contracts to ちゃ (吸っちゃだめ). It bans, whereas なければなりません compels.
caution: This forbids an action ("must not"); it is not the opposite of "must" — the opposite of なければなりません ("must do") is なくてもいい ("don't have to"), not this.

peg.ja: 逃げちゃダメだ。
peg.source: エヴァンゲリオン (Evangelion) — Shinji

nuance: The ながら action is the secondary one; the main action comes second.

examples.en: I cook while listening to music.
I study Japanese while watching anime.
I eat dinner while watching TV.

caution: Both actions must have the SAME subject and run at once — you can't use ながら when two different people act, and it attaches to the ます-stem (見ながら, not 見るながら).

peg.ja: 歌いながら、ギターを弾く。
peg.source: けいおん！(K-On!) — Yui (styled)

connection: V-た + ことがあります

examples.en: I have seen Mt. Fuji before.
I have been in the hot springs at Asahidake.
I've eaten sushi once before.

nuance: States life experience — "have (ever) done X before" (日本に行ったことがあります). It needs the past た-form and implies a notable, countable past occasion, so it fits unusual experiences, not everyday routine.
caution: Reserve it for genuine past experience, not recent/normal actions — 昨日食べたことがあります is odd; and it takes the た-form (食べたことがある), never the dictionary form.

peg.ja: この川に来たことがあります。
peg.romaji: Kono kawa ni kita koto ga arimasu.
peg.en: I've been to this river before.
peg.source: 千と千尋の神隠し (Spirited Away) — Chihiro (styled)

meaning: had better / it'd be better to
connection: V-た + ほうがいい(です); negative advice = V-ない + ほうがいい
nuance: Affirmative advice uses the PAST form (寝た), negative uses ない (寝ないほうがいい).

examples.en: You'd better take an umbrella.
You'd better go to bed early.
You'd better go home early.

caution: Affirmative advice uses the PAST た-form (寝たほうがいい), which feels counter-intuitive; negative advice uses ない (寝ないほうがいい) — using 寝るほうがいい for advice is the classic slip.

peg.ja: 警察を呼んだほうがいい。
peg.romaji: Keisatsu o yonda hō ga ii.
peg.source: 名探偵コナン (Detective Conan) — Conan (styled)

connection: plain or polite clause + から
nuance: Don't confuse with てから (after doing) — this から attaches to a reason clause.

examples.en: It's hot, so please open the window.
Tomorrow is an early start, so I'm going to bed now.
Because it's raining, I'll take an umbrella.

caution: This から means "because" and attaches to a full clause; don't confuse it with てから ("after doing") or the から that marks "from" a starting point/place.

peg.ja: 眠いから、寝るゾ。
peg.en: I'm sleepy, so I'm going to sleep.
peg.source: クレヨンしんちゃん (Crayon Shin-chan) — Shinnosuke (styled)

examples.en: I plan to go to Hokkaido over the summer holiday.
I intend to still be in Japan next year.
I intend to go back to my home country next year.

nuance: States a firm intention or plan the speaker has already settled on (来年帰るつもりです). It attaches to the dictionary form; the negative 〜ないつもり means "intend not to", distinct from 〜つもりはない ("have no intention").
caution: Use the dictionary form (行くつもり), not the ます-stem or past; and つもり expresses the speaker's own resolve — it's awkward for stating a third person's plans as fact.

peg.ja: 火影になるつもりだ！
peg.source: NARUTO — Naruto (styled)
